question: GaryClaw Phase 4b, cherry-pick #3. CONFIG VALIDATION ON RELOAD: When the file watcher detects a change to daemon.json, validate the new config before applying it. If the new config is invalid (bad cron expression, missing fields, etc.), keep running with the old config and log a warning + send a notification. Without this, a typo in the config file crashes the daemon. ~15 LOC. Effort: XS. Risk: Low. This is a robustness fundamental — production daemons should never crash on config edit.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Validate before applying. Invalid config = keep old + warn. ~15 LOC.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not worth it — users should test their config.

rationale: A daemon that crashes on a config typo is a bug, not a missing feature — this is squarely in the blast radius of config hot-reload and costs 15 LOC.
oracleRationale: A daemon that crashes on a config typo is a bug, not a missing feature — this is squarely in the blast radius of config hot-reload and costs 15 LOC.

question: GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. Cross-model disagreement on catchUpLimit. The review said catchUpLimit guards against future sub-minute poll intervals. The outside voice says it's dead code — the algorithm calls prev() once per tick, so it can NEVER fire more than once. The limit field is impossible to use as designed. Either: (A) remove the field entirely (simpler, honest), (B) fix the algorithm to support it (iterate prev() to find N missed windows, fire up to catchUpLimit). Worth investigating further?

options.label: A) Remove catchUpLimit field
options.description: Delete dead config. Catch-up always fires exactly once. Simpler and honest.

options.label: B) Fix algorithm to support limits > 1
options.description: Iterate prev() to find N missed windows. More complex but the field works as documented.

options.label: C) Add to TODOS.md
options.description: Keep the field for now, document it fires exactly once. Fix later if needed.

rationale: Dead code is a lie in the codebase — the field promises behavior the algorithm cannot deliver, and removing it is the 10-line obvious fix over building complexity to support a feature nobody has asked for.
oracleRationale: Dead code is a lie in the codebase — the field promises behavior the algorithm cannot deliver, and removing it is the 10-line obvious fix over building complexity to support a feature nobody has asked for.

question:
GaryClaw Phase 4b on main. Cross-model disagreement on fs.watch. The review used fs.watch as primary + mtime polling as safety net. The outside voice says mtime polling every 5s IS the real implementation, and fs.watch is unreliable dead weight on macOS. Since the daemon already has a 5s processNext() tick, mtime polling is trivially cheap and reliable. fs.watch's only advantage is sub-second detection, which doesn't matter for config reload (nobody needs config applied in <1s).

RECOMMENDATION: Choose A — drop fs.watch entirely. Simpler code, more reliable, no platform quirks. The 5s mtime poll is sufficient.

Completeness: A=9/10, B=7/10

options.label: A) Drop fs.watch, use mtime-only polling (Recommended)
options.description: Poll mtime on every processNext() tick (5s). Simpler, reliable, no platform quirks.

options.label: B) Keep both (fs.watch primary + mtime fallback)
options.description: fs.watch for sub-second detection, mtime as fallback. More complex but potentially faster.

chosen: A) Drop fs.watch, use mtime-only polling (Recommended)
rationale: fs.watch adds platform-specific complexity for a sub-second advantage nobody needs — mtime polling on the existing 5s tick is simpler, reliable, and sufficient.
oracleChoice: A) Drop fs.watch, use mtime-only polling (Recommended)
oracleRationale: fs.watch adds platform-specific complexity for a sub-second advantage nobody needs — mtime polling on the existing 5s tick is simpler, reliable, and sufficient.
